nativeRange.habitat: The Panther Grouper is found near lagoon and seaward reefs, often in areas with dead coral or silty substrates, at depths typically ranging from 2 to 40 m.

diet.notes: Accepts a variety of meaty marine foods including silversides, shrimp, crab, krill, squid, mussels, and marine pellets. Has been known to consume any fish or invertebrate small enough to fit in its mouth. Juveniles generally benefit from more frequent feedings (2–3 times daily), while adults tend to do well with feedings 2–3 times per week.

avoidWith: species small enough to be swallowed

imageCandidates.notes: Jim the Photographer (James Capaldi), 5472x3648. Tack-sharp full-body side view; greenish-white body with bold black polka-dot spots and humpback profile matching the species description exactly.
Bernard DUPONT, 2048x1376. Sharp in-situ wild reef shot in Indonesia showing whole fish in natural habitat with bold black spots; good variety vs. the aquarium shots.
Bjoertvedt, 3888x2592. Sharp full-body aquarium shot, browner colour morph with clear black spots and humpback profile; different angle for variety.

summary: The Panther Grouper is an Indo-Pacific species recognized by its white to greenish-white body covered in bold black spots. Generally considered less aggressive than most groupers, it has been known to consume any tankmate small enough to fit in its mouth. A very large aquarium with robust filtration is essential.
careNotes:
The Panther Grouper is generally considered one of the more manageable grouper species for home aquariums, but it still demands a very large tank — a minimum of 300 gallons (approximately 1,136 L) is widely recommended to accommodate its adult size and high bioload. Adult size in the wild can reach around 70 cm, though captive specimens tend to top out around 50–60 cm. Water quality must be carefully maintained, as this species is sensitive to poor conditions; an oversized protein skimmer and powerful filtration are commonly advised, with filter maintenance needed more frequently than a comparably sized reef system.

Like all groupers, the Panther Grouper is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning individuals are born female, and larger, dominant specimens may transition to male as they age. This transition has been documented in the wild but is rarely observed in captivity.

The Panther Grouper tends to be shy when first introduced to a new tank. Providing plenty of caves and overhangs made from live rock can help it settle in. As it grows, it has been known to appreciate more open swimming space.

Note on invasive range: Specimens have been documented off Florida's Atlantic coast and in the Gulf of Mexico, likely representing escaped or released aquarium fish. Aquarists should never release this species into non-native waters.
breedingNotes: The Panther Grouper is a protogynous hermaphrodite: individuals begin life as females, and larger, dominant specimens have been known to transition to male. Captive breeding has not been reliably achieved. The only documented captive spawning attempt, in 1979, produced fry that did not survive beyond one week. Successful reproduction in home aquariums is not considered a realistic prospect.

additional.notes: Temperature 75–78°F (23.9–25.6°C), salinity 1.022–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, tank size 100 gal minimum (likely for juveniles; outlier vs. 300 gal consensus for adults). Care level: easy to moderate.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Tank size 300 gal, temperature 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), salinity 1.021–1.023, care level easy, temperament aggressive. Adult size 20 inches in captivity.

fish.breedingNotes: The Panther Grouper is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as females, with larger dominant specimens capable of transitioning to male. Captive spawning has been documented only once (1979), with fry that did not survive. Successful breeding in home aquariums is not considered achievable.
